title: EXPOSÉ XI COHOMOLOGY OF COMPLETE INTERSECTIONS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XI COHOMOLOGY OF COMPLETE INTERSECTIONS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XI
COHOMOLOGY OF COMPLETE INTERSECTIONSby P. DELIGNE

pages.title: 1. Cohomology of projective space and of complete intersections

blocks.html: 1. Cohomology of projective space and of complete intersections

Let \(X\) be a complex non-singular projective variety, purely of dimension \(n\), and let \(\mathcal O(1)\) be an ample invertible sheaf on \(X\). By Akizuki and Nakano [1], it follows from the vanishing theorem of Kodaira [4] that
\[(*)\qquad H^i(X,\Omega_X^j(-1))=0\quad\text{for }i+j<n. \]

Again by [1], this result implies the "weak Lefschetz theorem", in complex cohomology. We propose to show, by brute force, that if \(X\) is a projective space over a field of arbitrary characteristic, then (*) remains true. This will allow us, in imitation of [1], to compare the Hodge and De Rham cohomologies of complete intersections, over a field of arbitrary characteristic, with those of projective space.

We then spell out some consequences of the results obtained. In n° 2, we present qualitative results deduced from the numerical formula of Hirzebruch (2.2). I wish to thank N. Katz for the help he gave me in the conception of this section.

1.0. Let \(E\) be a locally free module of rank \(r+1\) on a scheme \(S\). We suppose that \(r>0\). Recall that \(\mathbb P(E)\) denotes the relative projective space over \(S\) of hyperplanes of \(E\). We will denote by \(p\) the projection of \(\mathbb P(E)\) onto \(S\) and by \(\eta\in H^0(S,R^1p_*(\Omega^1_{\mathbb P(E)/S}))\) the first Chern class, Hodge style, of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O(1)\). Recall that \(\eta\) is the image under the logarithmic differential \(df/f:\mathcal O^*\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\) of the class of \(\mathcal O(1)\) in \(H^0(S,R^1p_*\mathcal O^*)\). For every sheaf \(F\) of \(\mathcal O\)-modules on \(\mathbb P(E)\), we set
\[ F(n)=F\otimes\mathcal O(1)^{\otimes n}\qquad(n\in\mathbb Z). \]

blocks.title: Theorem 1.1.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1.

(i) The sheaves \(R^ip_*(\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n))\) are locally free, and their formation is compatible with any base change.

(ii) For \(0\leq i\leq r\), the section \(\eta^i\) of \(R^ip_*(\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S})\) defines an isomorphism between this sheaf and \(\mathcal O_S\). Moreover,

\[ R^ip_*\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}=0\qquad\text{for }i\ne j\text{ or }i>r. \]

(iii) If \(n\ne0\), then, apart from the two following cases, \(R^ip_*(\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n))\) is zero:

  1. \((iii_a)\) \(i=0\) and \(n>j\)
  2. \((iii_b)\) \(i=r\) and \(n<j-r\).

Let \(V(E)=\operatorname{Spec}(\operatorname{Sym}^*E)\) be the affine space defined by \(E\), \(e\) its zero section, \(V(E)^*\) the complement \(V(E)^*=V(E)-e(S)\), \(a\) and \(a'\) the projections of \(V(E)\) and \(V(E)^*\) onto \(S\), and \(q\) the projection of \(V(E)^*\) onto \(\mathbb P(E)\):
\[ \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ V(E) & V(E)^* \ar[l] \ar[r]^q \ar[d]^{a'} & \mathbb P(E)\\ & S \ar[ul]^e \ar[ul]_a \ar[ur]_p & } \end{xy} \]

The punctured affine space \(V(\mathcal O(1))^*\) over \(\mathbb P(E)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(V(E)^*\), so that, for every quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal O\)-modules \(F\) on \(\mathbb P(E)\), we have canonically
\[(1.1.1)\qquad q_*q^*F\simeq\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}F(n).\]
This grading of \(q_*q^*F\) can be deduced from the action, on this sheaf, of the group \(\mathbb G_m\) of homotheties of \(V(E)\).

The relative vector fields on \(V(E)\) are identified with the \(S\)-morphisms from \(V(E)\) to \(V(E)\) (identified with the tangent space at the origin). The vector field \(X\) defined by the identity map of \(V(E)\) is inva- riant under homotheties; this vector field is tangent to the fibers of \(q\) and defines a trivialization
\[(1.1.2)\qquad X:\Omega^1_{V(E)^*/\mathbb P(E)}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{V(E)^*}.\]
If the \(x_i\) form a basis of \(E\), we have
\[X=\sum x_i\,dx^i.\]
Since \(\Omega^1_{V(E)^*/\mathbb P(E)}\) is of rank one, the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow q^*\Omega^1_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\longrightarrow \Omega^1_{V(E)^*/S}\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{V(E)^*/\mathbb P(E)}\longrightarrow0\]
induces exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow q^*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\longrightarrow \Omega^i_{V(E)^*/S}\longrightarrow \Omega^1_{V(E)^*/\mathbb P(E)}\otimes q^*\Omega^{i-1}_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\longrightarrow0.\]
These can be rewritten (1.1.2)
\[(1.1.3)\qquad 0\longrightarrow q^*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\longrightarrow \Omega^i_{V(E)^*/S}\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}} q^*\Omega^{i-1}_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\longrightarrow0.\]
We have canonically
\[(1.1.4)\qquad \Omega^i_{V(E)/S}=a^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^i E.\]
The short exact sequences (1.1.3) are thus obtained by splitting up a long exact sequence on \(V(E)^*\)

\[(1.1.5)\qquad \cdots\longrightarrow a'^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}}a'^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{i-1}E \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}}a'^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{i-2}E \longrightarrow\cdots.\]
This long exact sequence is none other than the restriction to \(V(E)^*\) of the Kozul resolution of \(\mathcal O_e(S)\) on \(V(E)\).
Let us project the long exact sequence (1.1.5) onto \(\mathbb P(E)\) by the affine morphism \(q\). By virtue of (1.1.1) and the formula \(q_*a'^*=q_*q^*p^*\), we obtain a long exact sequence

\[(1.1.6)\qquad \cdots\longrightarrow\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}} \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{i-1}E(n)\longrightarrow\cdots.\]
This one splits up into short exact sequences, images of (1.1.3),

\[(1.1.7)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n) \longrightarrow\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}} \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\Omega^{i-1}_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)\longrightarrow0.\]
The exact sequences (1.1.7) are sums of exact sequences

\[(1.1.8)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n) \longrightarrow p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n-i) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}}\Omega^{i-1}_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n) \longrightarrow0.\]
Recall (EGA III 2.1.15 or FAC) that the \(R^ip_*\mathcal O(k)\) are locally free, of formation compatible with any base change, and that
\[(1.1.9)\qquad R^ip_*\mathcal O(k)=0\quad\text{if }i\ne0,r,\text{ or if }i=0,\ k<0, \text{ or if }i=r,\ k>-r-1,\]
and that \(R^ip_*p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^jE(k)\) is therefore zero under these same conditions.
The exact sequences (1.1.8) provide a resolution

\[(1.1.10)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{E}p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n-i) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}}\cdots \longrightarrow p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^0E(n-0)\longrightarrow0\]
of \(\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)\). The spectral sequence defined by this resolution degenerates (\(E_1=E_\infty\)) for degree reasons (1.1.9); for \(0<j<r\), it yields

\[(1.1.11)\qquad R^jp_*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)= \mathcal H\{\cdots\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}} p_*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{i-j}E(n-i+j) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{X\mathbin{\lrcorner}}\cdots\}.\]
Since \(r\geq1\), a depth argument shows that

\[(1.1.12)\qquad \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}p_*p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n) =p_*q_*q^*p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE =a'_*a'^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE =a_*a^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE.\]

so that \(p_*p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n)\) is the homogeneous part of weight \(n+i\) of \(a_*a^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE\), that is \(\operatorname{Sym}^n(E)\otimes\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE\). If we recall that (1.1.5) comes from the Koszul resolution, we find by (1.1.11) that, for \(0<j<r\), \(R^jp_*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)\) is non-zero only if \(i-j=0\) and \(n-i+j=0\), i.e. if \(n=0\) and \(i=j\). In this case, moreover, \(R^ip_*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_S\).
For \(j=0\), we again deduce from (1.1.10) that

\[(1.1.13)\qquad p_*\Omega^i_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(X\mathbin{\lrcorner}:p_*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^iE(n-i) \longrightarrow p_*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{i-1}E(n-i+1)\bigr),\]
and the same argument shows that \(X\mathbin{\lrcorner}\) is injective when \(n-i\leq0\).

By virtue of (1.1.8) for \(n=0\), \(i=r+1\), we have \(\Omega^r_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\simeq p^*\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{r+1}E(-r-1)\). When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, the assertions of vanishing of cohomology groups in 1.1 (ii) and (iii) follow from what precedes for \(j\ne r\), and are deduced from it by duality ([2] V 11.2 p. 211) for \(j=r\). In particular, for any \(j\) and \(n\), there exists at most one \(i\) such that \(R^ip_*\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}(n)\ne0\), which implies (i) since \(\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\) is flat over \(S\) (the hardest part is finding the reference in EGA III; I suggest: 6.10.5 + 7.4.1 + 7.5.5 + a passage to the limit).

Knowing that the \(R^ip_*\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}\) are invertible sheaves for \(0\leq i\leq r\), it only remains to prove that, for \(S\) the spectrum of a field, we have \(\eta^i\ne0\). Indeed \(\eta^r\) is the cohomology class of a rational point (intersection of \(r\) hyperplanes), hence a basis of \(H^r(\mathbb P^r,\Omega^r)\) (FGA exp. 149 n°4).
1.2. It is possible to compute, directly from (1.1.10) or (1.1.13), the Euler–Poincaré characteristics of the \(\Omega^i(n)\). The result, not very appetizing, is
\[\chi(\mathbb P_k^r,\Omega^j_{\mathbb P_k^r}(n))= \sum_{a\leq i\leq j}(-1)^{i-j}\binom{r+1}{i}\binom{r+n-i}{r}.\]

blocks.title: Proposition 1.3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3. Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a proper smooth morphism, \(W\) a locally free sheaf of rank \(c\) on \(X\), \(F\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), \(d\) an integer \(>0\) and \(s:W\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) a section of \(W^\vee\). We suppose that \(S\) is noetherian and that

\(a\) The subscheme \(H\) of \(X\) defined by the vanishing of \(s\) is smooth over \(S\).

\(b\) Locally on \(X\), if the \(s_i\) are the coordinates of \(s\) in a basis of \(W^\vee\), the \(s_i\) form an \(\mathcal O_X\)-regular and \(F\)-regular sequence.

\(c\) For every sequence of integers \(k_i\), not all zero, we have

\[R^if_*\left(\bigotimes_i\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^{k_i}W\otimes\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F\right)=0 \quad\text{for }i+j<d.\]

Then we have:

\[(i)\qquad R^if_*\bigl(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F\bigr) \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}R^if_*\bigl(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F\bigr) \quad\text{for }i+j<d-c,\] \[(ii)\qquad R^if_*\bigl(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F\bigr) \longrightarrow R^if_*\bigl(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F\bigr) \quad\text{for }i+j=d-c.\]
The conormal sheaf \(N_{H/X}\) of \(H\) in \(X\) is isomorphic to the restric- tion of \(W\) to \(H\), so that the exact sequence
\[(1.3.1)\qquad0\longrightarrow N_{H/X}\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H \longrightarrow\Omega^1_{H/S}\longrightarrow0\]
defines a decreasing filtration of \(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\) whose successive quotients are given by
\[(1.3.2)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^k(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H) \simeq\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^kW\otimes\Omega^{j-k}_{H/S}.\]
It follows from \(b\) that we again have
\[(1.3.3)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^k(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F) =\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^kW\otimes\Omega^{j-k}_{H/S}\otimes F.\]
We will prove (i) and (ii) by induction on \(j\), simultaneously for all the \(F\) satisfying \(b\) and \(c\). These assertions are empty for \(j<0\);
let us therefore suppose (i) and (ii) proved for \(j'<j\). The induction hypothesis applies to the sheaves \(\bigwedge^kW\otimes F\); it provides, by \(c\):
\[(1.3.4)\qquad R^if_*\bigl(\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^kW\otimes\Omega^{j-k}_{H/S}\otimes F\bigr)=0 \quad\text{for }k>0\text{ and }i+j<d-c+k.\]
The above filtration of the \(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F\) defines a spectral sequence
\[E_1^{p,i-p}=R^if_*\bigl(\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^pW\otimes\Omega^{j-p}_{H/S}\otimes F\bigr) \Longrightarrow R^if_*\bigl(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F\bigr).\]
By (1.3.4), we have \(E_1^{p,i-p}=0\) if \(p>0\) and \(i+j\leq d-c\), whence we deduce that

\[(1.3.5)\quad\left\{\begin{aligned} R^if_*(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F)&\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}R^if_*(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F)&\quad&i+j<d-c,\\ R^if_*(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F)&\longrightarrow R^if_*(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F)&&i+j=d-c. \end{aligned}\right.\]

By the hypothesis \(b\), for a suitable differential, the \(\bigwedge^kW\otimes\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F\) form a left (Koszul) resolution of \(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F\). This filtration defines a spectral sequence
\[(1.3.6)\qquad E_1^{-p,q}=R^qf_*(\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^pW\otimes\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F) \Longrightarrow R^{q-p}f_*(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_H\otimes F).\]
By the hypothesis \(c\), we have \(E_1^{-p,q}=0\) for \(p>0\) and \(q+j<d\), or, trivially, for \(p\notin[0,c]\). Consequently,

\[(1.3.7)\quad\left\{\begin{aligned} R^if_*(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F)&\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}R^if_*(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F)&\quad&i+j<d-c,\\ R^if_*(\Omega^j_{X/S}\otimes F)&\longrightarrow R^if_*(\Omega^j_{H/S}\otimes F)&&i+j=d-c. \end{aligned}\right.\]
Assertions (i) and (ii) now follow from (1.3.5) and (1.3.7).

1.4. Let \(E\) be a sheaf of locally free modules of rank \(r+1\) on a scheme \(S\), \(\mathbb P(E)\) the corresponding projective bundle, \(d\) an integer \(\leq r\) and \(\underline a=(a_1,\ldots,a_d)\) a sequence of integers. A relative complete intersection in \(\mathbb P(E)\), of multidegree \(\underline a\), is a subscheme of \(\mathbb P(E)\), fiber by fiber of codimension \(d\), which, locally on \(S\) for the étale topology, is an intersection of \(d\) hypersurfaces of respective degrees \(a_1\cdots a_d\).

By virtue of 1.1, the hypotheses \(b\) and \(c\) of 1.3 are satisfied for \(X/S=\mathbb P(E)/S\), \(F=\mathcal O_X\), \(W=\sum_i\mathcal O(-a_i)\) and for \(s\) a section of \(W^\vee\) defining a relative complete intersection \(Y\subset\mathbb P(E)\) of multidegree \(\underline a\).

blocks.title: Theorem 1.5.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.5. Under the hypotheses of 1.4, let \(n=r-d\) be the relative dimension of \(Y\) and \(\eta\in H^0(S,R^1f_*\Omega^1_{Y/S})\) the first Chern class of \(\mathcal O(1)\)
\[\begin{xy}\xymatrix{Y\ar[r]\ar[dr]_f&\mathbb P(E)\ar[dl]^g\\&S}\end{xy}\]

If \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\), then:

(i) The \(R^if_*\Omega^j_{Y/S}\) are locally free, and their formation is compatible with any base change.

(ii) The spectral sequence \(E_1^{pq}=R^qf_*\Omega^p_{Y/S}\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}f_*(\Omega^\bullet_{Y/S})\) relating Hodge and De Rham cohomology degenerates (\(E_1=E_\infty\)), its abutment is locally free and its formation is compatible with any base change.

(iii)a We have

\[R^if_*\Omega^j_{Y/S}=0\quad\text{if }i\ne j\text{ and }i+j\ne n.\]

(iii)b \(\eta^i\) is a basis of \(R^if_*\Omega^i_{Y/S}\) if \(2i<n\).

(iii)c if \(n<2i\leq2n\) and if \(\alpha_i\) is the basis of \(R^if_*\Omega^i_{Y/S}\) dual to the basis \(\eta^{n-i}\) of \(R^{n-i}f_*\Omega^{n-i}_{Y/S}\), then

\[\eta^i=\left(\prod_1^d a_i\right)\alpha_i.\]

(iii)d If \(n=2m\), then \(\eta^m\ne0\) (if \(S\ne\varnothing\)) and the quotient \(R^mf_*\Omega^m_{Y/S}/\mathcal O_S\cdot\eta^m\) is locally free.

It suffices to prove 1.5 in a "universal" case, which allows us to assume \(S\) smooth over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\). In this case, assertion (ii) follows from (i), for it is true over \(\mathbb C\), hence over any field of characteristic 0, by Hodge theory. Moreover, \(S\) being reduced, (i) follows from (iii), assumed proved for \(S\) the spectrum of a field: indeed, by (iii), for each \(j\) the functions
\[s\longmapsto\dim_{k(s)}\bigl(H^i(Y_s,\Omega^j_{Y_s})\bigr)\]
are constant on \(S\), except perhaps for \(i=j\), hence are all constant since their alternating sum is (EGA III 7.9.2), and we conclude by EGA III 6.10.5 + 7.6.9 + 7.7.5.
By virtue of (1.4) and (1.3), we have

\[(1.5.1)\quad\left\{\begin{aligned} R^if_*\Omega^j_{Y/S}&\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}R^ig_*\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}&\quad&i+j<n,\\ R^if_*\Omega^j_{Y/S}&\longrightarrow R^ig_*\Omega^j_{\mathbb P(E)/S}&&i+j=n. \end{aligned}\right.\]

If \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, assertions (iii)a, (iii)b, (iii)d follow from (1.5.1) and from Serre duality ([2] V 11.2 p. 211). The cohomology class \(\eta^n\) is the cohomology class of a 0-cycle of degree \(\prod_1^d a_i\) (the degree of the complete intersection), so that
\[\eta^n=\left(\prod_1^d a_i\right)\alpha_n.\]
By definition, we have \(\eta^{n-i}\alpha_i=\alpha_n\), and (iii)c follows.
Assertions (i) and (ii) are therefore true, and assertions (iii), in the universal case, follow from (i) and from (iii) assumed proved for \(S\) the spectrum of a field.

blocks.title: Theorem 1.6.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.6. Keep the hypotheses and notation of 1.4, 1.5, suppose \(Y\) smooth, let \(\ell\) be a prime number prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), and denote by \(\eta_\ell\in H^0(S,R^2f_*(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)))\) the first \(\ell\)-adic Chern class of \(\mathcal O(1)\) (SGA 5 VII). Then

(i) \(R^if_*\mathbb Z_\ell=0\) if \(i\) is odd and distinct from \(n\).

(ii) If \(2i<n\), then \(R^{2i}f_*\mathbb Z_\ell(i)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z_\ell\), and admits \(\eta_\ell^i\) as a basis.

(iii) If \(n<2i\leq2n\), then \(R^{2i}f_*\mathbb Z_\ell(i)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z_\ell\), and admits \(\eta_\ell^i/\prod_1^d a_i\) as a basis.

(iv) The \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell\) is twisted-constant constructible without torsion and, if \(n\) is even, then \(\eta_\ell^{n/2}\ne0\) (if \(S\ne\varnothing\)) and \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(n/2)/\mathbb Z_\ell\cdot\eta_\ell^{n/2}\) is torsion-free.

The \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves \(R^if_*\mathbb Z_\ell\) are twisted-constant constructible, and their formation is compatible with any base change, by SGA 4 XVI 2.2. It therefore suffices to prove 1.6 for \(S\) the spectrum of an alge- braically closed field. An iterated application of SGA 4 XIV 3.3 then shows, for every \(m\), that the canonical arrow
\[H^q_Y\bigl(\mathbb P(E),\mathbb Z/\ell^m\bigr)\longrightarrow H^q\bigl(Y,\mathbb Z/\ell^m\bigr)\]
is bijective for \(q>r+d\) and surjective for \(q=r+d\). From this, and from Poincaré duality (SGA 4 XVIII), we deduce (SGA 5 VII) that the restriction arrow
\[H^q\bigl(\mathbb P(E),\mathbb Z_\ell\bigr)\longrightarrow H^q(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

is bijective for \(q<n\), injective with torsion-free cokernel for \(q=n\). This proves (i) for \(i<n\), (ii) and (iv). One verifies as in 1.5 that \(\eta_\ell^n\) is \(\prod_1^d a_i\) times the canonical class, and (i) and (iii) are then obtained by Poincaré duality (SGA 4 XVIII).

1.7. If \(Y\) is a complete intersection of dimension at least 3 in a projective space \(\mathbb P^r(k)\) over a field \(k\), it is known that \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z\), and is generated by \(\mathcal O(1)\) (SGA 2 XII 3.7). In dimension 2, and if \(Y\) is smooth, one can still prove:

blocks.title: Theorem 1.8.
blocks.html: Theorem 1.8. Let \(Y\) be a smooth surface which is a complete intersection in the projective space \(\mathbb P^r(k)\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then, \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(Y)=0\), and the class of \(\mathcal O(1)\) is not divisible in the Néron–Severi group \(\operatorname{NS}(Y)\).

Since \(H^1(Y,\mathcal O)=0\) (1.5 (iii)a), we know that \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(Y)=0\). The vanishing of \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(Y)\) then follows from the well-known lemma below, from the fact that \(H^2(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) is torsion-free (1.6 (iv)) and from the fact that \(H^0(Y,\Omega^1_Y)=0\) (1.5 (iii)a).

blocks.title: Lemma 1.9.
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9. Let \(Y\) be a complete algebraic variety over an algebraically closed field \(k\).

(i) If \(\ell\) is prime to the characteristic of \(k\), then the \(\ell\)-torsion of \(\operatorname{NS}(Y)\) is identified with the \(\ell\)-torsion of \(H^2(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\).

(ii) If \(k\) is of nonzero characteristic \(p\) and if \(Y\) is smooth, then the kernel of multiplication by \(p\) in \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is identified with the subgroup of \(H^0(Y,\Omega_Y^1)\) consisting of the locally logarithmic differentials.
The Kummer exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow\mu_{\ell^n}\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{x\mapsto x^{\ell^n}}\mathbb G_m\longrightarrow0\]
define exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(Y)/\ell^n\operatorname{Pic}(Y) \longrightarrow H^2(Y,\mu_{\ell^n})\longrightarrow (H^2(\mathbb G_m))_{\ell^n}\longrightarrow0,\]
where \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)/\ell^n\operatorname{Pic}(Y)=\operatorname{NS}(Y)\otimes\mathbb Z/\ell^n\). Passing to the projective limit, we find

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{NS}(Y)\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell \longrightarrow H^2(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell,H^2(\mathbb G_m))\longrightarrow0,\]
and the cokernel is torsion-free since \(\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell\) is divisible. Assertion (i) follows.
To prove (ii), it suffices to note that the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y^*\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{x\mapsto x^p} \mathcal O_Y^*\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{df/f}\Omega_Y^1\]
defines a short exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y^*\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y^* \longrightarrow\Omega^1_{Y\,\mathrm{loc\ log}}\longrightarrow0\]

and to take the corresponding long exact cohomology sequence. This argument is due to Cartier, who proves moreover that, if we denote by \(C\) the Cartier operation on \(H^0(Y,\Omega_Y^1)\), then the locally logarithmic differentials form the kernel of \(C-\operatorname{Id}\).

Suppose, by way of contradiction, that the class of \(\mathcal O(1)\) in \(\operatorname{NS}(Y)\) is of the form \(nu\), with \(n>1\). If \(\ell\) divides \(n\) and is prime to the characteristic, we again have, in \(\ell\)-adic cohomology
which contradicts 1.6 (iv).
Finally, if the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\) is different from 0, and if \(p\) divides \(n\), we again have, in Hodge cohomology
which contradicts 1.5 (iii)d and completes the proof.
2. Numerical results

2.1. Let \(\underline a=(a_i)_{1\leq i\leq d}\) be a sequence of integers \(\geq1\), and denote by \(V_n(\underline a)\) a smooth complete intersection of dimension \(n\) and of multidegree \(\underline a\) in a projective space \(\mathbb P^{n+d}\) over a field \(k\). We will set
\[h^{pq}(\underline a)=\dim_k H^q(V_{p+q}(\underline a),\Omega^p)\]
and
\[h_0^{pq}(\underline a)=h^{pq}(\underline a)-\delta_{p,q}.\]

The \(h_0^{pq}(\underline a)\) are thus the Hodge numbers of the middle-dimensional primitive cohomology of a complete intersection of multidegree \(\underline a\). They depend only on \(\underline a\), \(p\) and \(q\) (1.5). We will set
\[(2.1.1)\qquad H(\underline a)=\sum_{p,q\geq0}h_0^{p,q}(\underline a)y^pz^q\quad\in\mathbb Z[[y,z]].\]
For \(d=1\) and \(\underline a=(a)\), we set \(h^{pq}(a)=h^{pq}(\underline a)\) and \(H(a)=H(\underline a)\). For \(d=0\), we set \(\sup(a_i)=1\).
The following formula is the special case \(k=0\) of formula (2) p.160 of Hirzebruch [3]
\[(2.2)\qquad \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\sum_p\chi(V_n(\underline a),\Omega^p)y^pz^{n+d} =\frac1{(1+yz)(1-z)}\prod_{i=1}^d \frac{(1+yz)^{a_i}-(1-z)^{a_i}}{(1+yz)^{a_i}y+(1-z)^{a_i}}.\]
We shall transform this formula into the following one:

blocks.title: Theorem 2.3.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3. (Hirzebruch). With the notation of 2.1, we have
\[H(\underline a)=\frac1{(1+y)(1+z)}\left[ \prod_{1\leq i\leq d}\frac{(1+y)^{a_i}-(1+z)^{a_i}} {-(1+y)^{a_i}z+(1+z)^{a_i}y}-1\right].\]
By virtue of 1.5, we have
\[\chi(V_n(\underline a),\Omega^p)=(-1)^p+(-1)^{n-p}h_0^{p,n-p}(\underline a)\]
so that the first member I of (2.2) can be rewritten

\[\begin{aligned} I&=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\sum_{p+q=n}\bigl((-1)^p+(-1)^q h_0^{p,q}(\underline a)\bigr)y^pz^{p+q+d}\\ &=\sum_{p,q\geq0}(-1)^p(yz)^pz^qz^d +\sum_{p,q\geq0}(-1)^q h_0^{p,q}(\underline a)(yz)^pz^qz^d\\ &=z^d\left[\frac1{(1+yz)(1-z)}+H(\underline a)(yz,-z)\right]. \end{aligned}\]
The second member II of (2.2) can be rewritten
so that, cancelling \(z^d\) in \(I=II\), we find that
\[H(\underline a)(yz,-z)=\frac1{(1+yz)(1-z)}\left[ \prod_1^d\frac{(1+yz)^{a_i}-(1-z)^{a_i}} {(1+yz)^{a_i}z+(1-z)^{a_i}yz}-1\right]\]
and 2.3 is deduced from this formula by a change of variable.

blocks.title: Corollary 2.4.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4. With the notation of 2.1,

(i) for \(d=1\), we have

\[H(a)=-\frac{(1+y)^{a-1}-(1+z)^{a-1}} {(1+y)^az-(1+z)^ay} =\frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i,j\geq0}\binom{a-1}{i+j+1}y^iz^j} {\displaystyle1-\sum_{i,j\geq1}\binom a{i+j}y^iz^j}.\]

(ii) we have, \(|P|\) denoting the number of elements of a subset \(P\) of \([1,d]\),

\[H(\underline a)=\sum_{\substack{P\subset[1,d]\\P\ne\varnothing}} (1+y)^{|P|-1}(1+z)^{|P|-1}\prod_{i\in P}H(a_i).\]
By (2.3), we have
Let \(N\) and \(D\) be the numerators and denominators of this formula. We have \(H(a)=-N/D\),
\[N=\sum_k\binom{a-1}k(y^k-z^k) =(y-z)\sum_{i,j\geq0}\binom{a-1}{i+j+1}y^iz^j\]
and

\[\begin{aligned} D&=\sum_k\binom ak(y^kz-yz^k) =(z-y)+yz\sum_{k\geq1}\binom ak(y^{k-1}-z^{k-1})\\ &=(y-z)\left(-1+yz\sum_{i,j\geq0}\binom a{i+j+2}y^iz^j\right)\\ &=(y-z)\left(-1+\sum_{i,j\geq1}\binom a{i+j}y^iz^j\right) \end{aligned}\]
and assertion (i) follows.
Let us invert the formula of 2.3, for \(d=1\). We find
If we substitute this formula into 2.3, we obtain

\[\begin{aligned} H(\underline a)&=\frac1{(1+y)(1+z)}\left[ \prod_1^d\bigl(1+(1+z)(1+y)H(a_i)\bigr)-1\right]\\ &=\frac1{(1+y)(1+z)}\left[ \sum_{\substack{P\subset[1,d]\\P\ne\varnothing}} (1+z)^{|P|}(1+y)^{|P|}\prod_{i\in P}H(a_i)\right], \end{aligned}\]
whence (ii) follows.

blocks.title: Theorem 2.5.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.5. With the notation of 2.1

(i) If \(p+q=p'+q'\) and if \(p\leq p'\leq q'\leq q\), then

\[h_0^{pq}(\underline a)\leq h_0^{p'q'}(\underline a).\]

(ii) If \(p\leq q\), in order that \(h_0^{pq}(\underline a)\ne0\), it is necessary and sufficient that

\[p>\left[\frac{p+q+d-\sum a_i}{\sup(a_i)}\right].\]

where \([\ ]\) denotes the integral part of the quotient.
Let (*) be the following property of a formal power series
\[P=\sum a_{pq}y^pz^q:\]
(*) the \(a_{pq}\) are positive, \(a_{pq}=a_{qp}\), and if \(p+q=p'+q'\), and \(p\leq p'\leq q'\leq q\), then \(a_{pq}\leq a_{p'q'}\).
Assertion (i) follows at once from the formulas (2.4), from the identity \((1-x)^{-1}=\sum_{n\geq0}x^n\), and from the following lemma, whose proof is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma 2.5.1.
blocks.html: Lemma 2.5.1. The set of formal power series satisfying (*) is stable under addition and multiplication.
We will first prove (ii) in the special case of hyper- surfaces (\(d=1\)). The formula to be proved can then be rewritten, for \(p\leq q\),
\[(2.5.2)\qquad h_0^{pq}(a)\ne0\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p\geq\left[\frac{p+q+1}{a}\right]\] \[\left(\Longleftrightarrow\quad(p+1)a\geq p+q+2\quad\text{trivially}\right).\]
From the second formula of 2.4 (i), we deduce that \(h_0^{pq}\) is nonzero if and only if there exist pairs \((e,f)\) and \((c_i,d_i)\) such that
\[(2.5.3)\qquad\left\{\begin{aligned} (e,f)&\geq(0,0)\quad\text{and}\quad e+f\leq a-2,\\ (c_i,d_i)&\geq(1,1)\quad\text{and}\quad c_i+d_i\leq a,\\ (p,q)&=(e,f)+\sum(c_i,d_i). \end{aligned}\right.\]
For given \(p+q\), the smallest possible \(p\) is obtained for \(e=0\) and \(c_i=1\), and is the number of indices \(i\). This \(p\) is thus the infimum of the \(p\) for which we have
\[p\geq\frac{(p+q)-(a-2)}a,\quad\text{i.e.}\quad(p+1)a\geq p+q+2.\]

Let us pass to the general case. If \(\underline a'\) is deduced from \(\underline a\) by deleting the \(a_i\) equal to 1, then \(H(\underline a)=H(\underline a')\); in view of the form of the formula 2.5 (ii), we may assume that for every \(i\), \(a_i>1\). If \(d=0\), then \(h_0^{pq}=0\) and the assertion is correct; there is no loss of generality in assuming that \(d>0\) and that \(a_d=\sup(a_i)\).

By virtue of 2.4 (ii), and (2.5.2), in order that \(h_0^{pq}(\underline a)\ne0\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a nonempty subset \(P\) of \([1,d]\), a pair \((e,f)\) and pairs \((c_i,d_i)_{1\leq i\leq d}\) such that
\[(2.5.4)\qquad\left\{\begin{aligned} (|P|-1,|P|-1)&\geq(e,f)\geq(0,0),\\ c_i,d_i&\geq\left[\frac{c_i+d_i+1}{a_i}\right];\quad(c_i,d_i)\geq(0,0),\\ (p,q)&=(e,f)+\sum(c_i,d_i). \end{aligned}\right.\]

For fixed \(n=p+q\), let us look for the smallest possible value of \(p\). If \(n_i=c_i+d_i\), for such a \(p\), the conditions (2.5.4) can be rewritten, in terms of the \(c_i\), \(n_i\), \(e\) and \(f\)

\[(2.5.5)\qquad\left\{\begin{aligned} a_i=2&\Longrightarrow n_i\ \text{even}\qquad(\text{otherwise (2.5.4) is impossible}),\\ c_i&\geq\left[\frac{n_i+1}{a_i}\right];\qquad c_i\leq n_i,\\ (|P|-1,|P|-1)&\geq(e,f)\geq(0,0),\\ n&=e+f+\sum n_i,\\ p&=e+\sum c_i. \end{aligned}\right.\]
This description shows first of all that the smallest \(p\) is obtained for \(P=[1,d]\), the case to which we will restrict ourselves. If we have

then there exists a solution with \(p=c_i=e=0\). Otherwise, if \(a_d e2\), the best we can do is to take \(n_i=a_i-2\) for \(i e d\), so that \(c_i=0\) for \(i e d\), \(e=0\), \(f=d-1\) and \(n_d=n-(d-1)-\sum_{i e d}(a_i-2)\). We then have
\[(2.5.7)\qquad p=\left[\frac{n-(d-1)-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-2)+1}{a_d}\right],\]
and thus, for \(p\leq q\), we have \(h_0^{pq}(\underline a)\ne0\) if and only if
\[(2.5.8)\qquad p\geq\left[\frac{n-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)+1}{a_d}\right].\]

The formula (2.5.8) reduces to (2.5.2) if \(d=1\) and is trivially true if (2.5.6) is satisfied. If \(d\geq2\), if (2.5.6) is not satisfied, and if all the \(a_i\) are equal to 2, the best we can do is again to take \(n_i=0\) (\(i\ne d\)), \(e=0\), \(f=d-1\) or \(d-2\), congruent to \(n\) modulo 2, and \(n_d=n-f\). If \(n\equiv d-1\pmod2\), we prove (2.5.7) as above. Otherwise, we note that
\[\left[\frac{n-(d-2)+1}{2}\right]= \left[\frac{n-(d-1)+1}{2}\right]\]
so that (2.5.7), and thereby (2.5.8), is valid even if \(a_d=2\).
It remains to check that (2.5.8) is equivalent to the formula 2.5 (ii). Indeed, we have
\[\left[\frac{n-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)+1}{a_d}\right] =\left[\frac{n+d-\sum_{i\ne d}a_i}{a_d}\right] =\left[\frac{p+q+d-\sum a_i}{a_d}\right]+1.\]

2.6. If \(X\) is a complete intersection of dimension \(n\) in \(\mathbb P^r\), it follows at once from (2.5.8) that the primitive part of \(H^n(X)\) vanishes only if \(X\) is a quadric of odd dimension.
Definition 2.7. If \(X\) is a proper and smooth algebraic variety over \(\mathbb C\), the Hodge level of \(H^n(X)\) is the supremum of the numbers \(q-p\) for \(p+q=n\) and \(h^{pq}(X)\ne0\).
The Hodge level \(\ell\) is thus congruent to \(n\) modulo 2, and is at most equal to \(\inf(n,\dim(X)-n)\). It is \(\geq0\), except if \(H^n(X)=0\), in which case it is equal to \(-\infty\).

blocks.title: Corollary 2.8.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8. Let us take up the notation of 2.1 again, setting \(k=\mathbb C\), and assum- ing that \(a_d=\sup(a_i)\). Let \(\ell\) be an integer \(0\leq\ell<n\), congruent to \(n\) modulo 2. In order that the Hodge level of \(H^n(V_n(\underline a))\) be \(\leq\ell\), it is necessary and sufficient that
\[\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)\leq(\ell+1)-(a_d-2)\frac{n-\ell}{2}.\]
By virtue of (2.5.8), if the Hodge level is \(\geq0\), it is the supremum of 0 and of
\[n-2\left[\frac{n-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)+1}{a_d}\right]\]
and the inequality
\[n-2\left[\frac{n-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)+1}{a_d}\right]\leq\ell\]
can be rewritten
\[\frac{n-\ell}{2}\leq\left[\frac{n-\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)+1}{a_d}\right],\]
or again
\[\sum_{i\ne d}(a_i-1)\leq1+n-a_d\cdot\frac{n-\ell}{2} =\ell+1-(a_d-2)\frac{n-\ell}{2}.\]

2.9. With the help of formula 2.8, it is easy to enumerate, for small \(\ell\), the multidegrees of the complete intersections in \(\mathbb P^r\), of dimension \(n>1\), whose \(H^n\) is of level \(\ell\). The table given in the first version of this exposé was nevertheless incorrect, and the one given below is extracted from the more complete one given by M. Rapoport in his article: Complément à l'article de P. Deligne "La conjecture de Weil pour les surfaces K 3", Inventiones Math. 15 227–236 (1972). For \(\ell\leq1\), the complete intersections \(V_n(\underline a)\) of dimension \(n\) and of multidegree \(\underline a=\) \((a_1,\ldots,a_d)\), with \(n>\ell\), \(d\geq1\) and \(a_i\geq2\), of Hodge level \(\ell\), are the following:

\[ \begin{array}{rcll} \ell=-\infty && V_{2k+1}(2) & (k\geq0),\\[4pt] \ell=0 && V_{2k}(2)\ (k\geq1),\quad V_{2k}(2,2)\ (k\geq1),\quad V_2(3),\\[4pt] \ell=1 && V_{2k+1}(2,2)\ (k\geq1),\quad V_{2k+1}(2,2,2)\ (k\geq1),\\ && V_3(3),\quad V_5(3),\quad V_3(3,2),\quad V_3(4). \end{array} \]
2.10. For small \(\underline a\), the generating functions of the \(h_o^{pq}(\underline a)\) can be written, by 2.4
so that
BIBLIOGRAPHY

Lefschetz theorems - Proc. Jap. Acad. 30, 266-272, (1954).
[2] R. HARTSHORNE. Residues and duality. Lecture notes 20 Springer 66.

[3] F. HIRZEBRUCH. Topological methods in algebraic geometry
3rd ed. Springer-Verlag 1966.

[4] K. KODAIRA. On a differential geometric method in the theory of
analytic stacks. Proc. Nat. Acad. Sci. USA 39, 1268-1273, (1953).
